items.question: Is the business problem formulated in ML terms (classification, regression, ranking, etc.)?
Was a root-cause analysis of the problem conducted (e.g., "Five Whys" or "inverted pyramid" method)?
Is the business context of how the task arose described (current state of affairs, main business scenarios, and description of "pain points")?
Is the task type formulated in machine-learning terms (e.g., fraud classification, regression for pricing, or ranking for search)?
Was an analysis of the existing process (flow analysis) conducted?
Is the impact of the existing process on business metrics (KPIs) and the estimate of potential benefit described?
Is an estimate of the potential benefit from implementing the new system described?
Is it described what the system must NOT do?
Are the system's goals described (what it must do)?
Has this task been solved before?
What methods were used (e.g., heuristics, expert judgment, or simple moving windows)?
Why did the current solution stop satisfying the business/users?
Are technical risks described (e.g., lack of infrastructure)?
Are business risks described?
Are business risks quantified in money? Is there a calculation of potential losses?
Are constraints/limitations described?
Are non-functional requirements listed (latency SLA, cost of error, security)?
Is a risk model described (worst-case scenarios to avoid)?
Are metrics split into online (business metrics) and offline (technical) ones?
Is the chosen loss function differentiable and suitable for optimizing the algorithm?
Is a hierarchy of metrics built (linking proxy metrics to final goals)?
Are all data sources (internal and external) listed with metadata?
Is the data filtering process and handling of missing values/duplicates described?
Is a data labeling strategy defined (team, platform, quality control)?
Does the validation scheme match how the model is applied in reality (e.g., time-based split for time series)?
Is an independent test set ("golden set") set aside for final evaluation?
Is data leakage ruled out (e.g., by grouping on user ID)?
Is a constant baseline (median, mean, or random answer) described for comparison?
Is a simple ML baseline (linear model or heuristic) proposed before moving to complex models?
Is feature importance evaluated, along with its contribution to model explainability?
Are tests for feature correctness described (value ranges, correlations)?
Is a hypothesis for A/B testing formulated (what changes, what it affects, expected effect)?
Is the minimum detectable effect (MDE) and experiment duration calculated?
Is a plan for analyzing learning curves provided (data size selection, overfitting detection)?
Is a plan for residual analysis and finding patterns in errors described?
Are scenarios for analyzing corner cases defined?
Are steps for ensuring reproducibility described (versioning of code, data, and configs)?
Are smoke tests provided for quickly checking that the pipeline works?
Is the API design described (request/response format, versioning)?
Is a fallback strategy defined in case the main model fails?
Is the release cycle described (blue-green or canary deployment)?
Are alerts set up for technical health (latency, errors) and for data drift?
Is prediction logging provided for later retraining and analysis?
Is a responsibility matrix (RACI) created for supporting the system after launch?
Is a runbook ("in case of fire" plan) prepared for on-call engineers?
Do the technical offline metrics directly correlate with the business KPIs stated in the goals?
Does the integration section provide fallback strategies specifically for the risks described at the start of the document?
Is the simplest baseline from the relevant section used as one of the fallback-strategy levels?
Do the features described in the Feature Engineering section correspond to available fields in the described data sources?
Do the metric thresholds considered "normal" during validation match the alert-triggering thresholds in monitoring?
